one_line_takeaway: SSG replaces KGW's random red/green vocabulary split with a logit-balanced pairwise split, provably raising the worst-case watermark-strength lower bound and empirically boosting detection TPR/F1 on low-entropy code and math generation without hurting Pass@1, but its gains partly evaporate when the original prompt is unavailable at detection time and its robustness to paraphrase attacks is inconsistent across models.
key_finding: With original prompts available, SSG consistently and often substantially improves detection metrics over the matching non-SSG baseline while Pass@1 stays roughly flat or improves slightly: e.g. on HumanEval with Qwen2.5-Coder-7B, KGW+SSG raises T@1 from 22.0 to 39.0 and F1@1 from 35.8 to 55.9 (Pass@1 25.6 vs 26.2 baseline); on GSM8K with DeepSeekMath-7B, KGW+SSG raises T@1 from 41.7 to 90.9 and F1@1 from 58.5 to 94.9 while Pass@1 rises from 21.7 to 25.8. The top-k ablation shows most of the detection gain is captured already at k=2, with k>2 giving only marginal further gains at the cost of decoding speed, motivating the paper's recommendation of k=2 or k=4. However, two results complicate the headline claim: (1) in the prompt-free (general-prompt) setting, SSG's effect on detection is explicitly described as 'mixed' -- e.g. KGW+SSG T@1 on HumanEval/Qwen only rises from 22.0 to 22.6 (vs. 39.0 with the original prompt), and on LLaMA-3-8B/HumanEval, KGW+SSG's T@1 (6.7) is actually lower than KGW alone (13.4); (2) under the GPT-4o-mini paraphrase attack, SSG's robustness is inconsistent across models -- e.g. EWD+SSG on GSM8K/DeepSeekMath-7B collapses to T@1=0.0/F1@1=0.0 versus EWD-alone's T@1=22.7/F1@1=36.8, a case where adding SSG actively hurts adversarial robustness, even though on LLaMA-3-8B/GSM8K EWD+SSG improves T@1 from 1.5 to 12.1 over EWD alone.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'Watermark Strength' (fws), a token-level metric quantifying the normalized shift in green-set probability mass caused by KGW-style logit biasing, and proves that under KGW's random vocabulary partitioning the lower bound of this quantity can be near zero whenever the top-m high-logit tokens happen to fall in the same (red or green) subset -- a non-negligible event in low-entropy generation (code, math) where only a handful of tokens carry most of the probability mass. Building on this analysis, the authors propose SSG (Sort-then-Split by Groups): at each generation step, sort vocabulary tokens by logit, form consecutive pairs (or top-k tokens only, for efficiency), and use a key-and-context-seeded hash to assign one token of each pair to the green set and one to red, so that green/red are logit-balanced by construction rather than by uniform random hashing. The remaining (non-top-k) tokens are split via the standard random KGW-style permutation. SSG is presented as a drop-in replacement for the vocabulary-partitioning step of any KGW-family injection scheme (the paper combines it with KGW, SWEET, and EWD as the underlying detection statistic) and is proven (Sec. 4.4) to raise the provable lower bound on fws relative to random partitioning, with the bound's tightness controlled by the top-1 token probability p1.
threat_model: This is a provenance/content-attribution mechanism paper, not an attack paper: the 'adversary' considered is (a) a downstream party trying to determine, via a statistical detection test (z-score threshold), whether a piece of text was generated by a specific watermarked LLM, motivated by providers' terms-of-service prohibiting training on their outputs and by concerns about AI-generated misinformation; and (b) in Section 5.5, an active adversary who paraphrases watermarked text with GPT-4o-mini in an attempt to erase the watermark signal before detection. The paper does not consider adaptive/white-box attackers, watermark key extraction, or spoofing/forgery attacks -- robustness is evaluated only against single-pass LLM paraphrasing.
limitations: The paper's own Limitations section states the evaluated scope is narrow (only code and math low-entropy domains, and it calls for extending to more datasets), and explicitly flags that SSG's improved injection reliability still depends on availability of the original prompt at detection time, restricting real-world applicability -- reflected empirically in the mixed/degraded prompt-free results in Tables 3-4. The paraphrase-robustness section (5.5) itself concedes robustness 'varies' across models and is 'poor' for DSMath-7B. The theoretical guarantee (Sec 4.4) only bounds fws away from zero when p1 (the top token's probability) is not extremely close to 1; the authors note that when a single token dominates, no partitioning scheme can substantially help. The Appendix C quality-preservation argument is a first-moment (expected logit shift) equivalence to KGW, not a proof of equivalent downstream text-quality distribution, and the paper acknowledges concurrent work (Park et al. 2026, 'WaterMod') proposes a very similar logit-balanced/paired-grouping partitioning idea, differing mainly in whether green/red colors are assigned independently per group (SSG) versus uniformly across groups (WaterMod); SSG additionally provides the theoretical fws lower-bound analysis that the paper claims WaterMod lacks.

quantitative_results: KGW+SSG vs KGW on HumanEval/Qwen2.5-Coder-7B: T@1 22.0->39.0, F1@1 35.8->55.9, Pass@1 26.2->25.6
KGW+SSG vs KGW on GSM8K/DeepSeekMath-7B: T@1 41.7->90.9, F1@1 58.5->94.9, Pass@1 21.7->25.8
EWD+SSG vs EWD on GSM8K/LLaMA-3-8B: T@1 74.2->99.2, F1@1 84.8->99.2
Prompt-free setting: KGW+SSG vs KGW T@1 on HumanEval/LLaMA-3-8B drops (13.4->6.7), showing SSG gain is prompt-dependent
Paraphrase attack: EWD+SSG vs EWD on GSM8K/DeepSeekMath-7B, T@1 collapses 22.7->0.0 (SSG hurts robustness in this case)
Paraphrase attack: EWD+SSG vs EWD on GSM8K/LLaMA-3-8B, T@1 improves 1.5->12.1
High-entropy C4 with LLaMA-3-8B: KGW+SSG vs KGW, T@1 81.0->93.0, PPL 3.01->2.80 (lower perplexity, i.e. better text quality, with SSG)
Top-k ablation: detection gains largely saturate at k=2; k>2 yields marginal further gains at increasing decoding-speed cost
